content.text: IDP Service Catalog for GenAI Components

An Internal Developer Portal (IDP) is the single pane of glass where engineers discover, onboard to, and monitor the platform components their services depend on. For GenAI teams, the catalog is especially valuable because the component landscape — LLM gateways, prompt registries, embedding stores, evaluation platforms, model registries — is novel, rapidly evolving, and often poorly documented. Engineers who cannot find or understand these components fall back on ad-hoc solutions that duplicate infrastructure, violate policy, and create hidden costs.

This sub-subtopic covers what a GenAI-oriented IDP service catalog should contain, how each component entry is structured, how the catalog integrates with policy enforcement, and how to keep catalog entries accurate as the platform evolves.

attrs.alt: IDP GenAI Service Catalog — Component Taxonomy

content.text: What Belongs in the GenAI Service Catalog

A service catalog entry exists for every platform component that a GenAI team might need to discover, configure, or depend on. The catalog is not a documentation site — it is a structured registry that supports search, dependency graphs, health status, and policy compliance checks. The following component types belong in a GenAI-specific catalog:
Component Type
Examples
Why It Belongs in the Catalog
LLM Gateway
Internal proxy to OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock; rate limiting; auth; routing rules
Teams need to know which gateway to use for which model and how to configure their service to route through it
Prompt Registry
Versioned prompt template store with approval workflow
Teams must not hard-code prompts in code; the registry is the approved source of truth for production prompts
Eval Platform
Automated eval runner, eval suite storage, score dashboards
Teams need to register their eval cases and trigger eval runs from CI; discovering the platform via catalog reduces setup time
Embedding Store
Vector database namespaces, index configurations, embedding model versions
Each service's RAG pipeline needs a namespace; the catalog manages namespace allocation and documents index schemas
Observability Hub
Trace collection endpoints, metrics namespaces, cost dashboards, alert routing
Teams configure their spans to route to the hub; the catalog documents the attribute schema and the dashboard URLs
Model Registry
Fine-tuned model artifacts, base model versions approved for use, lineage graphs
Teams deploying fine-tuned models need to register them; discovery of existing models prevents duplicate fine-tuning runs
Golden Path Templates
Cookiecutter / Backstage scaffolding for new GenAI services, pre-baked with CI/CD, eval gates, and policy config
The fastest path to a policy-compliant new service is a template; the catalog makes templates discoverable
Dependency Allowlist
Approved SDK tiers, active waivers, SBOM store
Teams can check allowlist status before adopting a new package; the catalog surfaces policy constraints at the point of discovery
Anatomy of a Catalog Entry
Each catalog entry follows a standard schema. Using Backstage (the most common open-source IDP platform) as the reference implementation, a GenAI component entry is a YAML file in the
catalog-info.yaml
format:

# catalog-info.yaml — LLM Gateway component entry
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: llm-gateway
  title: LLM Gateway
  description: |
    Enterprise proxy for LLM provider APIs. Routes requests to OpenAI,
    Anthropic, and AWS Bedrock with centralised authentication, rate limiting,
    cost attribution tags, and prompt logging for audit.
  annotations:
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
    github.com/project-slug: acme/llm-gateway
    pagerduty.com/service-id: P1234567
  tags:
    - genai
    - platform
    - llm
  links:
    - url: https://llm-gateway.internal/docs
      title: API Documentation
    - url: https://grafana.internal/d/llm-gateway
      title: Dashboard
    - url: https://runbooks.internal/llm-gateway
      title: Runbooks

spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:platform-team
  system: genai-platform

  # GenAI-specific extensions (custom annotations)
  providesApis:
    - llm-gateway-api

  # Policy compliance
  x-genai-policy:
    approved-providers: [openai, anthropic, aws-bedrock]
    auth-method: api-key-via-vault
    rate-limit-per-team: 1000 RPM
    cost-attribution: required
    prompt-logging: optional
The
x-genai-policy

block is a custom extension that the platform team adds to all GenAI component entries. It makes policy constraints discoverable at the catalog level, before a developer writes a single line of code. When a developer finds the LLM Gateway in the catalog, they immediately see which providers are approved, how authentication works, and that cost attribution tags are required on every request.
Structuring the Catalog for Discovery
A catalog that contains 50 component entries but requires engineers to scroll through all 50 to find what they need is not useful. Structure the catalog for discovery across three dimensions:
Tag-Based Filtering
All GenAI components receive the tag
genai
plus one or more capability tags:
llm
embedding
eval
observability
registry
template
. Engineers building a RAG pipeline can filter by
genai + embedding
to immediately see the embedding store options without wading through CI or monitoring components.
System Grouping
Group related components into a System entity in Backstage. All GenAI platform components belong to the
genai-platform
system. Services built on top of the platform belong to their product area's system (e.g.
customer-service-genai
). The system view renders a dependency graph that shows how components relate — essential for understanding blast radius when the LLM Gateway is updated.

# System definition — groups all platform components
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: System
metadata:
  name: genai-platform
  title: GenAI Platform
  description: Shared infrastructure for all GenAI services at Acme
  tags: [genai, platform]
spec:
  owner: group:platform-team
  domain: engineering
Golden Path Prominence

Templates (Golden Path scaffolding) should be the first thing a new GenAI team encounters in the catalog. Pin them to the catalog homepage. Label them clearly as "Start Here" for new service creation. A team that discovers and uses a Golden Path template before writing any code will inherit the correct eval gates, CI pipeline, observability config, and dependency policy automatically — dramatically reducing the platform team's time spent on code review and policy enforcement.
Catalog Entries for Each GenAI Component Type
Beyond the common entry schema, each component type has specific fields that make its entry actionable rather than just informative.
LLM Gateway Entry

Include: supported providers and models (with context limits), authentication setup (link to Vault path), rate limits per team tier, cost attribution tag requirements, and a code snippet showing the minimum viable configuration for a new service:

# Minimum viable LLM Gateway configuration (from catalog entry)
import httpx

LLM_GATEWAY_URL = "https://llm-gateway.internal/v1"

async def call_model(prompt: str, model: str = "gpt-4o") -> str:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            f"{LLM_GATEWAY_URL}/chat/completions",
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {VAULT_TOKEN}",
                "X-Cost-Attribution-Team": "my-team",      # Required
                "X-Cost-Attribution-Service": "my-service", # Required
            },
            json={
                "model": model,
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            },
            timeout=30.0,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
Prompt Registry Entry

Include: how to register a new prompt template (CLI or UI), the approval workflow (who must approve, what review criteria apply), how to reference a template version from application code, and how prompt version changes are propagated to dependent services:

# Referencing a versioned prompt from the registry
from prompt_registry import PromptRegistry

registry = PromptRegistry(base_url="https://prompt-registry.internal")

# Fetch the approved production version of a template
template = registry.get("customer-support-system-prompt", version="v3.2")

# Use in your service — version is pinned, changes require re-approval
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": template.render(product=product_name)},
    {"role": "user", "content": user_query},
]
Eval Platform Entry

Include: how to register eval cases (file format, tags, metadata), how to trigger eval runs from CI (API endpoint, authentication), where scores are stored and how to query them, and the threshold configuration format used by the CI eval gate:
Integrating Policy Enforcement with the Catalog

A service catalog that only provides documentation is a library. A catalog that actively enforces policy is a platform. GenAI components carry non-trivial policy requirements — approved providers, cost attribution tags, eval gate requirements, dependency allowlist compliance — and the catalog is the right place to surface and enforce them.
Policy Checks as Catalog Plugins

Backstage's plugin architecture allows the platform team to add policy check panels to each catalog entity page. These panels query the policy systems in real time and display the compliance status of each service:
Policy Check
Data Source
Display
Dependency allowlist compliance
CI allowlist gate results API
Green/Red badge; link to failing packages
Eval gate status
Eval platform last-run results
Pass rate percentage; link to failing cases
Cost attribution configured
LLM Gateway tag validation API
Yes/No; link to configuration guide
Prompt registry usage
Prompt registry API: are production prompts registered?
Coverage percentage; list of unregistered prompts
Active security waivers
Waiver registry API
Count; link to waiver details
Scorecard health
Scorecard API last-week summary
RAG status; link to full scorecard

When a developer views the catalog entry for their service, they see all policy checks inline. A Red dependency allowlist badge is a direct call to action — they click it, see which package is unapproved, and follow the link to the RFC template. The policy is enforced at the discovery layer, not just at the CI gate.
Blocking New Service Creation Without Template

The IDP's software templates feature can be configured to require catalog entry creation as part of new service scaffolding. When a developer creates a new GenAI service via the Backstage template, the template automatically:
Creates the repository with a
catalog-info.yaml
pre-populated with the service's name, owner, and system.
Registers the service in the IDP catalog.
Creates the eval platform namespace for the service's eval suite.
Configures the LLM Gateway cost attribution tag with the service name.
Opens a Slack notification to the platform team that a new GenAI service has been created.

This automation means every new GenAI service starts catalog-registered, with the minimum viable policy configuration in place. There is no "I'll add the catalog entry later" — the template makes the catalog entry the prerequisite for having a repository.
Dependency Graph and Blast Radius Analysis

One of the most valuable features of a service catalog is the dependency graph — the visual representation of which services depend on which platform components. For GenAI services, the dependency graph enables blast radius analysis: when the LLM Gateway is updated or the prompt registry goes down, which services are affected?
In Backstage, dependencies are declared in
catalog-info.yaml
using
dependsOn
and
consumesApis
fields:

# catalog-info.yaml — a GenAI service declaring its dependencies
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: customer-support-bot
  title: Customer Support Bot
spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:customer-service-team
  system: customer-service-genai

  dependsOn:
    - component:llm-gateway
    - component:prompt-registry
    - component:embedding-store
    - component:eval-platform

  consumesApis:
    - api:llm-gateway-api
    - api:prompt-registry-api

The IDP aggregates these declarations across all services and renders the dependency graph. The platform team can immediately see, when planning a LLM Gateway maintenance window, that 14 services depend on it — and notify their owners rather than surprising them with an outage.

Blast radius analysis becomes critical during provider incidents. When OpenAI experiences degraded availability, the platform team queries the catalog for all services that route through the LLM Gateway to OpenAI specifically. The catalog's routing configuration annotation (
approved-providers: [openai, anthropic]
) makes this query possible in seconds rather than requiring a manual audit.
Self-Service Onboarding Flows

The catalog is not just a discovery tool for existing components — it is the entry point for self-service onboarding to platform capabilities. Each component entry includes an onboarding section that walks a new team through the specific steps required to use that component:
LLM Gateway Onboarding
Request a team API key via the Vault self-service portal (link in catalog entry).
Add the
X-Cost-Attribution-Team
and
X-Cost-Attribution-Service
headers to all requests (code snippet in catalog entry).
Configure the gateway URL as an environment variable in your service's Kubernetes deployment (template in catalog entry).
Verify connectivity: run the provided curl test command from your staging environment.
Register your service in the gateway's rate-limit tier (form linked from catalog entry).
Eval Platform Onboarding
Create an eval suite namespace:
POST /api/v1/suites
with your service name and owner (API reference in catalog entry).
Write your first three eval cases in the
evals/
directory using the provided YAML schema.
Add the eval gate to your CI pipeline using the GitHub Actions snippet in the catalog entry.
Set your pass rate threshold in the eval gate configuration.
Run the first eval suite manually to establish a baseline score.

Self-service onboarding flows eliminate the bottleneck where every new team must wait for a platform engineer to walk them through setup. The platform team invests time once in writing the onboarding documentation inside the catalog entry; every subsequent team onboards independently in under an hour.
Keeping the Catalog Accurate

A catalog with stale entries is worse than no catalog — engineers who find outdated documentation waste time following instructions that no longer work, then lose trust in the catalog entirely. Three practices keep the catalog accurate:
Catalog-as-Code with CI Validation
Store all
catalog-info.yaml

files in the component's own repository. A CI job validates the YAML schema on every PR that touches the file. When the component changes its API, rate limits, or authentication method, the owning team updates the catalog entry in the same PR — the catalog entry is part of the component's definition, not a separate documentation task.

# .github/workflows/catalog-lint.yml
name: Catalog Lint
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['catalog-info.yaml']

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Validate catalog entry schema
        run: |
          pip install pyyaml jsonschema
          python scripts/validate_catalog_entry.py catalog-info.yaml             --schema .catalog-schema/genai-component.json
Health Status Integration
Each catalog entry integrates with the service's monitoring system to display a live health status. In Backstage, this is done via the
EntityStatusCard

component that queries the component's health endpoint or the observability hub. Engineers browsing the catalog see whether the LLM Gateway is currently healthy before they decide to depend on it — or choose to design around its availability SLA.
Quarterly Catalog Audit

The platform team audits every catalog entry once per quarter. The audit checks: Is the description still accurate? Are the linked runbooks and dashboards still reachable? Are the policy annotations current? Has the component's owner changed? Entries that fail the audit are flagged with a
"lifecycle": "deprecated"
or
"lifecycle": "experimental"
status until they are updated.
Summary

An IDP service catalog for GenAI components solves the discoverability problem that plagues teams working with novel, policy-heavy infrastructure. By cataloguing the LLM gateway, prompt registry, eval platform, embedding store, observability hub, model registry, golden path templates, and dependency allowlist as structured entries — with policy constraints, code snippets, health status, and dependency graphs — the catalog becomes the first stop for any engineer building a GenAI service. Catalog-as-code in component repositories, CI schema validation, health status integration, and quarterly audits keep entries accurate. The result is a platform that teams can use confidently without requiring a platform engineer to walk them through setup every time.
